title: Electrocatalytic Oxidation of Hydrazine at Glassy Carbon Electrode Modified with Electrodeposited Film Derived from Caffeic Acid
abstract:
Electrocatalytic Oxidation of Hydrazine at Glassy Carbon
Electrode Modi®ed with Electrodeposited Film Derived from
Caffeic Acid
S.M. Golabi* and H.R. Zare++
+ Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
++Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
Received: February 24, 1999
Final version: June 30, 1999
Abstract
A caffeic acid modi®ed glassy carbon electrode was constructed for the electrocatalytic detection of hydrazine. This modi®ed electrode
enabled hydrazine to be catalytically oxidized at a greatly reduced overpotential and in a wide operational pH range (pH 5.0±9.0). The
overall number of electrons involved in the catalytic oxidation of hydrazine and the number of electrons involved in the rate determining
step are 4 and 1, respectively. The rate constant for the catalytic reaction was evaluated by chronoamperometry, cyclic voltammetry and
RDE voltammetry and was found to be around 10ÿ3 cm sÿ1. Experiments were performed to characterize the electrode as an amperometric
sensor for the determination of hydrazine and linear calibration plots were obtained over the range of 0.01 to 2.0 mM for linear sweep
voltammetry and chronoamperometry. Amperometry in stirred solution exhibits a linear dynamic range of 2.5 mM to 1.0 mM (correlation
coef®cient 0.9993) and a detection limit of 0.4 mM. The resulting modi®ed electrode retains its initial response for at least 2 weeks if stored
in phosphate buffer (pH 4). The precision of amperometry was found to be 1.8 % for replicate determinations of a 1.0 mM solution of
hydrazine (n  12).
Keywords: Caffeic acid, Electrocatalytic oxidation, Hydrazine, Glassy carbon, Modi®ed electrode
1. Introduction
In practice, hydrazine is widely used in some industrial
applications as catalyst, emulsi®er, corrosion inhibitor and
reducing agent. It is also used as an oxygen scavenger in industry
and has found wide application as an antioxidant, photographic
developer and an insecticide [1]. Hydrazine is very important in
pharmacology because it has been recognized as a carcinogenic
and hepatotoxic substance, which affects liver and brain glu-
tathione [2], so its detection has attracted considerable analytical
interest. In addition, electrooxidation of hydrazine is the basis of
an established fuel cell, and hence is a reaction of practical
importance, due to its high capacity and no contamination [3].
Consequently, the detection of trace hydrazine is very important
in environmental and biological analysis [4]. Numerous methods
have been reported for the determination of trace amounts of
hydrazine, including titrimetric [5], coulometric [6], potentio-
metric [7, 8], spectrophotometric [1, 9, 10] and chromatographic
methods [11±13]. Electroanalytical techniques have been shown
to provide a selective and sensitive approach, based on electro-
oxidation of hydrazine, for the detection of hydrazine and its
derivatives. Unfortunately, because of the large overpotential of
hydrazine at conventional electrodes, it is not well suited for
quantitation via conventional electrochemical approaches. One
promising approach for minimizing overvoltage effects and
facilitating the determination is through the use of a electro-
catalytic process at chemically modi®ed electrodes. Previous
efforts to enhance the amperometric detection of hydrazine
included the application of a pre-anodized glassy carbon elec-
trode [14]. Some chemically modi®ed electrodes were con-
structed with good catalytic activity toward the electrooxidation
of hydrazine, including cobalt phthalocyanine modi®ed carbon
paste electrode [4, 15], cobalt tetraphenylporphyrin [16], cobalt
protoporphyrin dimethyl ester [17] and oxymanganese ®lm
modi®ed electrodes [18], inorganic mixed-valent Prussian blue
[19, 20] and its analogous ruthenium cyanide [21], nickel ferri-
cyanide [22] and cobalt hexacyanoferrate [23] modi®ed glassy
carbon electrodes. Also, chemically modi®ed electrodes prepared
by electropolymerization of 3,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde [24, 25]
and electrodeposition of chlorogenic acid [26] on the glassy
carbon electrode exhibit an electrocatalytic response toward
hydrazine compounds.
However, a detailed investigation about the electrochemical
properties of electrodes modi®ed with naturally occurring qui-
nones seems to be interesting in order to explore their reactivity
as mediator toward the catalysis of slow electron transfer reac-
tions and elucidate the relation that exists between their structures
and reactivities. In this direction, we described recently the pre-
paration of a caffeic acid modi®ed glassy carbon electrode and its
application in the electrocatalytic oxidation of NADH. [27]. The
present work demonstrates that a glassy carbon electrode coated
with an electrodeposited ®lm of caffeic acid, enables the elec-
trooxidation of hydrazine to proceed effectively at signi®cantly
low potential and in a wide pH range. Moreover, owing to the
linear dependence of hydrazine oxidation current to its con-
centration in a wide dynamic range, the possibility of hydrazine
quantitation in stirred and unstirred solutions was shown using
amperometry and voltammetry, respectively.
2. Experimental
2.1. Apparatus
All electrochemical experiments were carried out using a 746
VA electrochemical analyzer equipped with a 747 VA Stand
three-electrode cell both from Metrohm. This con®guration
contains a glassy carbon (GC) working electrode, a platinum-
wire auxiliary electrode and a silver-silver chloride (with 3.0 M
potassium chloride) electrode served as the reference electrode.
1293
Electroanalysis 1999, 11, No. 17
# WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
1040±0397/99/1711±1293 $17.50.50=0
 All potentials were reported with respect to this reference elec-
trode.
2.2. Chemical and Solutions
Hydrazine (as hydraziniumhydroxide) and caffeic acid (CFA)
or 3,4-dihydroxycinnamic acid were obtained from Merck and
Riedel-de Haen, respectively, and used as received. All other
chemicals used for buffer solutions preparation were of analytical
grade. All solutions were prepared with doubly distilled water.
Caffeic acid and hydrazine solutions were prepared just prior to
use.
2.3. Procedure
Prior to electrode modi®cation, the glassy carbon electrode
surface was polished with 0. 05 mm alumina suspension using a
polishing cloth and then rinsed with doubly distilled water and
acetone. This electrode was activated in 0.1 M sodium bicarbo-
nate solution by continuous potential cycling from ÿ1.1 to 1.6 V
at scan rate of 100 mV sÿ1 until a stable voltammogram was
obtained. Afterward, the activated electrode was rinsed with
doubly distilled water and was modi®ed by cycling the potential
between 0.1 and 0.9 V at 20 mV sÿ1 for 12 minutes in 1.0 mM
solution of CFA in 0.15 M phosphate buffer (pH 4). During this
stage, a monolayer of CFA is covalently bound to the functional
groups, which are produced in the surface of electrode in the
course of its electrochemical treatment. The stability of such
modi®ed electrode was examined by successive monitoring of its
response to a 1.0 mM solution of hydrazine in phosphate buffer
(pH 7.5). Our results show a maximum decrease of 14 % in peak
current after 300 cycles of potential between ÿ100 to 500 mV
versus ref. On the other hand, the response of modi®ed electrode,
stored for 14 days in phosphate buffer (pH 4), to a 1.0 mM
solution of hydrazine decreased around 2 %. These results permit
us to conclude that the CFA modi®ed electrode is suf®ciently
stable and can be considered as an electrode with reproducible
responses. All experiments were carried out at ambient tem-
perature. N2 bubbling was used to remove oxygen from the
solution in the electrochemical cell.
3. Results and Discussion
3.1. Electrocatalytic Oxidation of Hydrazine at the
Caffeic Acid Modi®ed Glassy Carbon Electrode
The electrochemical response of a caffeic acid modi®ed glassy
carbon electrode was previously reported and a mechanism was
also proposed for electrode surface modi®cation [27]. Figure 1
shows the cyclic voltammograms of the bare and caffeic acid
modi®ed glassy carbon electrode in the absence and presence of
hydrazine. The caffeic acid modi®ed glassy carbon electrode in
0.15 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.5), without hydrazine in solution,
exhibits a well-behaved redox reaction (Fig. 1a). Upon the
addition of 1.0 mM hydrazine, there is a dramatic enhancement
of the anodic peak current and the cathodic peak current dis-
appeared completely (Fig. 1b), which indicates a strong electro-
catalytic effect. The anodic peak potential for the oxidation of
hydrazine at CFA modi®ed electrode is about 250 mV (Fig. 1b),
while at the bare glassy carbon electrode no current is observed
in the presence of hydrazine (Fig. 1d). The bare GC electrode is
not reactive under these conditions while the activated bare GC
electrode shows an anodic peak but at more positive potentials
and with low peak current (Fig. 1e). This is in agreement with the
fact reported in the literature [20, 29] that hydrazine oxidation is
observed on GC electrodes only after special pretreatment of the
surface.
Figure 2 shows the dependence of the voltammetric response
of CFA modi®ed glassy carbon electrode on the hydrazine con-
centration. With the addition of hydrazine, there was an increase
in the anodic current. Inset A of Figure 2 shows clearly that the
plot of Ip versus hydrazine concentration constituted from two
linear segments with different slopes, corresponding to two dif-
ferent ranges of substrate concentration. This discrepancy in the
slope did not arise from the probable adsorption of substrate at
the electrode surface, since the Q versus t1=2 plots obtained from
the analysis of chronocoulograms recorded for modi®ed elec-
trode in the absence and in the presence of hydrazine exhibit the
straight lines with equal intercepts [31]. Accordingly, we ascribe
this to a change in catalytic reaction conditions arising from the
Fig. 1. Catalytic oxidation of hydrazine at a modi®ed glassy carbon
electrode in 0.15 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) a) without hydrazine and
b) with 1.0 mM hydrazine at a scan rate of 25 mV sÿ1. c) as (a) and d) as
(b) for bare glassy carbon electrode. e) as (b) for activated bare glassy
carbon electrode.
1294
S. M. Golabi, H. R. Zare
Electroanalysis 1999, 11, No. 17

However, a variation of peak potential smaller than 60 mV per
unit of pH is observed. Such a discrepancy is also observed and
reported by other workers in the case of electrocatalytic oxidation
of hydrazine and attributed to have a kinetics rather than the
Figure 3B shows the calibration plots of hydrazine catalytic
oxidation at CFA modi®ed electrode obtained at various solution
pH. As can be ascertained from these curves, for pH values lower
than about pH  pKa, a decrease in the sensitivity, slope of the
calibration plots, is observed. Indeed, at this range of pH,
hydrazine will be present in its protonated form and this might be
responsible for the observed effect. Although the slopes of the
calibration plots increase with increasing pH, the changes are not
dramatic for pH values above 7. In addition, the linear range of
calibration plots remained virtually unchanged. Moreover, with
increasing pH values, a decrease in the surface coverage is also
observed (Fig. 3C, curve a), which may be due to the poor sta-
bility of the CFA ®lm in higher pH. The results obtained here are
generally in agreement with those reported in the literature [30].
However, it should be noted that this apparent decrease in the
surface coverage does not involve a dramatic decrease in the
sensitivity of the modi®ed electrode for catalytic oxidation of
hydrazine, as can be ascertained from the calibration plots and
the coverage-normalized responses, i.e. I=G in different pH
illustrated in Figure 3B and C, curve b, respectively. The increase
of coverage-normalized response with increasing pH, suggests
that despite the decrease of surface coverage, the modi®er exhi-
bits a nearly high and constant activity. These results suggest a
more effective interaction of hydrazine with the o-quinone groups
of the CFA ®lm at slightly basic or neutral pH with respect to the
3.3. Chronoamperometric and Amperometric Studies
Chronoamperometric measurements of hydrazine at CFA
modi®ed glassy carbon electrode were done by setting the
working electrode potential at 330 mV and for increasing con-
centrations of hydrazine. It is evident that there is an increase in
the ®xed time current plots for increasing concentrations of
hydrazine. Figure 4 shows a plot of the ®xed time current, the
current measured after 45 s, as a function of hydrazine con-
centration in a 0.15 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.5).
Similarly to the case of cyclic voltammetry, this plot exhibits
obviously two separate linear correlations each corresponding to
a given concentration range. As mentioned above, we de®ned
these by G.E.U. and G.E.A zones for low and high concentrations
of hydrazine respectively. The inset of Figure 4 illustrates the
linear correlation between ®xed time current and hydrazine
concentrations in the range of 0.01 to 2 mM. For hydrazine
concentrations above 8 mM, the measured current is also linearly
proportional to the concentration, but with a decreased sensitivity
(slope). It seems that at higher substrate concentrations, the
catalytic oxidation of hydrazine occurs with some limitation with
constant effect. We suggest such a behavior, as mentioned above,
due to the effect of the nitrogen gas bubbles formation at the
electrode surface. Indeed, the change in the slopes of the cali-
bration plots arises from the change of the apparent diffusion
coef®cient of hydrazine, which is smaller when the electrode
surface is almost covered by nitrogen bubbles.
Chronoamperometry was used for measurement of the diffu-
sion coef®cient of hydrazine. For an electroactive material with a
diffusion coef®cient, D, the current for the electrochemical
reaction (under diffusion control) is described by the Cottrell
where D and cb are the diffusion coef®cient (cm2 sÿ1) and the
bulk concentration (mol cmÿ3), respectively. The plot of I versus
tÿ1=2 will be linear, and from the slope, the value of diffusion
coef®cient can be obtained. A mean value of 1.2610ÿ5 cm2 sÿ1
was calculated for D using the slopes of I±tÿ1=2 plots of hydra-
zine solutions with concentrations below 1 mM. This value cor-
responds to the diffusion coef®cient of hydrazine in aqueous
medium and is in good agreement with those reported by others
[32, 33]. Similarly, the diffusion coef®cient of hydrazine can be
estimated from the slopes of I±tÿ1=2 plots obtained from the
chronoamperograms of the concentrated solutions of hydrazine
10ÿ6 cm2 sÿ1 which is signi®cantly lower than that calculated for
low concentrations of the substrate. However, such a low value
seems to be reasonable if one considers the diffusion slackening
effect of the produced gas at the surface of electrode.
Also, the rate constant for the following chemical reaction, k0

according to the method described in [34]:

Fig. 4. Plot of chronoamperometric currents at 45 s as a function of
hydrazine concentration for a CFA modi®ed glassy carbon electrode in
0.15 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) at a potential step of 330 mV. G.E.U.:
gas evolution unaffected, G.E.A.: gas evolution affected. The inset shows
the I versus CN2H4 plot for low substrate concentrations.
where Ic is the catalytic current of modi®ed glassy carbon elec-
trode in the presence of hydrazine, IL is the limited current in the
absence of hydrazine and g  kcbt (cb is the bulk concentration of
hydrazine) is the argument of the error function. In the cases
where g exceeds 2, the error function is almost equal to 1 and the
above equation can be reduced to:
IC=IL  p1=2g1=2  p1=2khcbt1=2
The above equation can be used to calculate the rate constant of
the catalytic process, kh. Having measured the catalytic current, i.e.,
IC, it is possible to carry out the electrode process in identical
conditions, but in the absence of hydrazine, in order to determine IL.
Knowing the ratio IC=IL, one can simply calculate thevalue of kh for
a given concentrationof substrate from the slope of IC=IL versus t1=2
plot. Such a plot was constructed from the chronoamperograms of a
CFA modi®ed glassy carbon electrode in the absence and presence
of 0.04 mM hydrazine (not shown here). In these conditions, the gas
formation has no effect on the catalytic process and the electron
transferbetweenhydrazineandthesuper®cialsitesofmodi®eristhe
sole rate determining factor. The calculated value of kh is
2.16103 Mÿ1 sÿ1 using the slope of Ic=IL±t1=2 curve. This value of
kh explain well the sharp feature of the catalytic peak observed for
catalytic oxidation of hydrazine at the CFA modi®ed glassy carbon
electrode. On the other hand, we obtained the surface coverage (G)
of a modi®ed electrode prepared at optimum conditions from the
equation G  Q=nFA, where Q is the charge obtained by integrating
theanodic peak under thebackgroundcorrection, A isthegeometric
area of the electrode and other symbols have their usual meanings.
The calculated value of G was 4.8610ÿ10 mol cmÿ2 at pH 7.5.
Using this value of coverage, the heterogeneous rate constant of
catalytic reaction was calculated as k0
Amperometric experiments were implemented in stirred
solutions. The CFA modi®ed electrode potential was kept in
330 mV (versus ref.) during to successive addition of hydrazine.
The resulting current versus time curve for a series of replicate
concentrations of hydrazine at the lower range of 2.5 mM to
40 mM is shown in inset B of Figure 5. Figure 5 shows clearly
that the measured current increases slowly by increasing the
hydrazine concentration in solution. This can also be considered
as a result of gas evolution on the diffusion of hydrazine towards
the electrode surface. However, for low substrate concentrations,
(2.5 mM to 1.0 mM) the iss versus CN2H4 plot is reasonably linear
(Fig. 5, inset A) and reveals that the electrode can be used as an
amperometric sensor. Owing to the fact that amperometry under
stirred conditions, has a much higher current sensitivity than
cyclic voltammetry or chronoamperometry, it was used to esti-
mate the lower limit of detection for the electrocatalytic oxidation
of hydrazine at a CFA modi®ed glassy carbon electrode. A
calibration plot constructed from the data of inset B, give a limit
of detection of 0.4 mM according to the de®nition Yl:o:d 
YB  3sB. The average peak current (mA=mM) and the precision
estimated in terms of relative standard deviation (%RSD) for
twelve repeated measurements (n  12) of 1.0 mM hydrazine at
an applied potential of 330 mV, on a CFA modi®ed electrode was
26.59 0.47 mA=mM and 1.8 %, respectively.
3.4. Electrocatalytic Characteristics of Hydrazine
Oxidation at the CFA Modi®ed Electrode
As we previously reported [27] caffeic acid modi®ed glassy
carbon electrode exhibits electrochemical responses which are
characteristic of the redox species con®ned on the electrode
surface. The cyclic voltammetric response of the modi®ed elec-
trode had the expected wave shape for a surface-con®ned redox
center which the formal potential, taken as the average of anodic
and cathodic peak potentials, is about 175 mV and is almost
independent of the potential scan rate for sweeping rates below
400 mV sÿ1. In addition, the current was directly proportional to
the rate of potential scan over the range from 25 to 400 mV sÿ1,
suggesting facile charge transfer between the electrodeposited
CFA and the electrode. The addition of hydrazine to the solution
resulted in a dramatic changing of the voltammogram, with a
large enhancement of the anodic current and complete dis-
appearance of cathodic peak current. Figure 6A shows the cyclic
voltammograms of 1.0 mM hydrazine solution at different sweep
In order to get the information on the rate determining step, a
Tafel plot was drawn using the data derived from the rising part
of the current±voltage curve at scan rate 6 mV sÿ1 (curve d, Fig.
6A). A slope of 195.3 mV=decade is obtained indicating a one
electron transfer to be rate limiting assuming a transfer coef®-
cient of a  0.7 (Fig. 6B, curve a). The Tafel slope, b, can also be
obtained by another method. Figure 6B, curve b, shows that the
peak potential, Ep, is proportional to log v. The slope of Ep versus
log v plot is @ Ep= @ (log v)  95.8 mV. The Tafel slope may be
estimated according to the equation for a totally irreversible

from polarization measurements. This slope, indicates a one

It can also be noted in Figure 6A that with increasing the
sweep rate, the peak potential for the catalytic oxidation of
hydrazine shifts to more positive potentials, suggesting a kinetic
limitation in the reaction between the redox sites of the CFA ®lm
and hydrazine. However, the oxidation currents for hydrazine
increase linearly with the square root of the sweep rate (Fig.
6C, d), suggesting that at suf®cient overpotential the reaction is
mass transport controlled. Also, a plot of the sweep rate-nor-
malized current (Ip=v1=2) versus sweep rate (Fig. 6C, s), exhibits
the characteristic shape typical of an ECcat. process. These results
show that the overall electrochemical oxidation of hydrazine at
modi®ed electrode might be controlled by the cross-exchange
process between hydrazine and the redox site of the CFA ®lm and
the diffusion of hydrazine. Under these conditions, the Andrieux
and Saveant theoretical model [36] can be used to derive a
relation between the peak current and the concentration of
hydrazine for the case of slow scan rates, v, and large catalytic

In order to elucidate the kinetics of hydrazine oxidation at the
CFA modi®ed electrode, rotating disk electrode measurements
were also performed with different rotation speeds in 0.15 M
phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) containing various concentrations of
hydrazine. The Levich plots obtained at 300 mV are shown in
Figure 7A. As can be seen, the current increases with increasing
rotational speed, but was found to be nonlinear, including kinetic
limitations. The catalytic current, Icat, corresponding to the
mediated reaction, is a function of the Levich current, ILev,
representing the mass transfer of hydrazine in the solution and
the kinetic current, Ik, corresponding to the electron cross-
exchange between hydrazine and the CFA redox sites. The value
of catalytic reaction rate constant, k0

The caffeic acid modi®ed glassy carbon electrode ef®ciently
catalyses the oxidation of hydrazine. The optimal pH for the
catalytic process was found to be around 7.5. The currents
amperometry are diffusion controlled for low concentrations of
hydrazine, while at high substrate concentrations the currents are
signi®cantly decreased most probably because of nitrogen gas
evolution. The kinetic parameters such as electron transfer
coef®cient, a, and catalytic reaction rate constant, k0
determined using various electrochemical approaches. Voltam-
metry and amperometry can be used as analytical methods for
hydrazine determination in quiet and moving solution, respec-
